coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of g-C3N4/Ag2C2O4 heterojunction composite membrane with efficient visible light photocatalytic activity for water disinfection
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jiaxin
Lan, Xiuquan
Cheng, Jianhua
Zhou, Xinhui
coredata.dc:description: Water pollution, deriving from the contamination of pathogenic bacteria, has posed a threat to human's survival and development. Photocatalytic disinfection is being widely studied in decentralized drink water safety, as traditional disinfection technologies are limited by harmful disinfection by-product and excessive energy consumption. Herein, a novel composite membrane (PN/Ag) with plasmonic heterojunction was synthesized for the efficient photocatalytic disinfection through the combination of polyacrylonitrile (PAN), N-doped carbon dots (NCDs)/g-C3N4 and Ag2C2O4 by electrospinning technique and successive ionic layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) process. The surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of Ag nanoparticles and Schottky barrier formation between metal and semiconductor contributed to the efficient separation of electron-hole pairs and the generation of reactive species, resulting in outstanding photocatalytic disinfection of PN/Ag composite membranes (7.48 and 7.70 log inactivation of E. coli and S. aureus respectively in 80 min) and good reusability under visible light illumination. Moreover, the potential Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanisms were proposed for PN/Ag system according to the band structure and reactive species analysis. The as-proposed PN/Ag composite membranes may shed light on the design and application of materials in water purification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrospinning
Nanofiber membranes
Water disinfection
Visible light
Heterojunction photocatalysts

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of PN/Ag composite membranes.
SEM images of (a) NCDs/g-C3N4, (b) PAN, (c) PN, (d) PN/Ag-1, (e) PN/Ag-5, (f) PN/Ag-10.
(a) XRD pattern of NCDs/g-C3N4, Ag2C2O4, PAN/Ag-10 and PN/Ag and (b) FT-IR spectra of PAN, PN, Ag2C2O4, and PN/Ag.

(a) UV–vis DRS of NCDs/g-C3N4, Ag2C2O4, PAN, PN and PN/Ag and (b) plot of (αhv) 1/2 and (αhv)2 (inset) vs. photon energy (hv). Inset in (b) showing the corresponding band energy value of (1) NCDs/g-C3N4, (2) Ag2C2O4, (3) PN, (4) PN/Ag-1, (5) PN/Ag-5 and (6) PN/Ag-10.

Photocatalytic disinfection performance of as-prepared membranes for (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus under different conditions. Cycle disinfection experiment of (c) E. coli and (d) S. aureus. Photographs of (e) E. coli and (f) S. aureus colonies treated by different photocatalysts without and with visible light irradiation, respectively.
SEM images of (a, b) E. coli and (c, d) S. aureus before and after photocatalytic disinfection by PN/Ag, respectively.

Photocatalytic disinfection efficiencies of PN/Ag-10 with different scavengers for (a) E. coli and (b)S. aureus. ESR spectra of the (c) DMPO- ·OH and (d) DMPO- ·O2 − adducts recorded with the PN/Ag-10 under visible light irradiation. (e) PL spectra of PN, PN/Ag-1, PN/Ag-5, PN/Ag-10. (f) EIS of PN, PN/Ag-1, PN/Ag-5, PN/Ag-10.
Schematic illustrating the photocatalytic disinfection mechanism of PN/Ag composite membranes.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of g-C3N4/Ag2C2O4 heterojunction composite membrane with efficient visible light photocatalytic activity for water disinfection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Solid Wastes Pollution Control and Recycling, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, Guangdong, 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Solid Wastes Pollution Control and Recycling

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Solid Wastes Pollution Control and Recycling, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, Guangdong 510006, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: South China Institute of Collaborative Innovation, Dongguan, 523808, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: South China Institute of Collaborative Innovation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: South China Institute of Collaborative Innovation, Dongguan 523808, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Environment and Energy, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510006, China.
Corresponding author. South China Institute of Collaborative Innovation, Dongguan, 523808, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: South China Institute of Collaborative Innovation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water pollution, deriving from the contamination of pathogenic bacteria, has posed a threat to human's survival and development. Photocatalytic disinfection is being widely studied in decentralized drink water safety, as traditional disinfection technologies are limited by harmful disinfection by-product and excessive energy consumption. Herein, a novel composite membrane (PN/Ag) with plasmonic heterojunction was synthesized for the efficient photocatalytic disinfection through the combination of polyacrylonitrile (PAN), N-doped carbon dots (NCDs)/g-C3N4 and Ag2C2O4 by electrospinning technique and successive ionic layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) process. The surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of Ag nanoparticles and Schottky barrier formation between metal and semiconductor contributed to the efficient separation of electron-hole pairs and the generation of reactive species, resulting in outstanding photocatalytic disinfection of PN/Ag composite membranes (7.48 and 7.70 log inactivation of E. coli and S. aureus respectively in 80 min) and good reusability under visible light illumination. Moreover, the potential Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanisms were proposed for PN/Ag system according to the band structure and reactive species analysis. The as-proposed PN/Ag composite membranes may shed light on the design and application of materials in water purification.

ce:section.ce:para: Waterborne pathogens have always been hazardous to human health through causing various infectious diseases and even mass death, such as the current COVID-19 epidemic (Kraemer et al., 2020; Tian et al., 2020). According to the report of World Health Organization (WHO), contaminated water can transmit diseases such diarrhoea, cholera, dysentery, typhoid and polio. Even worse, contaminated drinking water is estimated to cause 485,000 diarrhoeal deaths each year (World Health Organization, 2019). Some traditional water disinfection technologies, including UV irradiation, chlorination and ozonization are usually used to kill the pathogenic microorganism (J. Chen, Loeb and Kim, 2017; Gopal et al., 2007; Shannon et al., 2008). However, it is difficult for these methods to avoid harmful disinfection by-product and excessive energy consumption (Susan D. Richardson, 2002). Hence, it is vital to develop eco-friendly and efficient disinfection materials and techniques.

Many photocatalytic materials have received attention due to their proper band gap and good performance in degradation of pollutants, such as graphitic carbon, bismuth tungstate and bismuth oxyhalides and so on (Amiri et al., 2020; Amiri et al., 2019; J. Liu et al., 2015). The photocatalytic water disinfection has been widely studied because it produces almost no disinfection by-products, highly inactivates pathogens and uses renewable solar energy (Fernandez-Ibañez et al., 2020; C. Liu et al., 2016; C. Zhang, Li, Shuai, Shen and Wang, 2019). Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has been considered to be a promising photocatalyst because of low cost and environmentally friendliness (J. Liu et al., 2015; Ong et al., 2016). However, the low visible light absorption efficiency and high recombination rates of photogenerated electron-hole pairs have become crucial issues for pure g-C3N4 (Ma et al., 2016). Furthermore, because of its dispersion in water, it is difficult to be recycled, which greatly hinders its practical applications. Recently, N-doped carbon dots (NCDs), which is a novel type of carbon-based nanomaterial, has been successfully combined with g-C3N4 to improve its photocatalytic activity by broadening the photo-adsorption region and decreasing electron-hole recombination (Sun et al., 2015). In addition, silver-based photocatalysts including Ag2CO3, Ag3PO4, Ag3VO4, AgI and AgCl have been used to couple with g-C3N4 to construct heterogeneous photocatalysts, which is also a widely way to avoid the photo-corrosion of silver-based photocatalysts and improve its photocatalytic activity (He et al., 2015; Y. F. Li et al., 2015; Orooji et al., 2020; S.-Z. Wu, Li and Zhang, 2015; Yu et al., 2014; S. Zhang et al., 2014). However, to our knowledge, Ag2C2O4 modified g-C3N4 has rarely been reported.

Herein, we present an intriguing strategy of PN/Ag composite membranes with plasmonic heterojunction, which was synthesized of polyacrylonitrile (PAN), NCDs/g-C3N4 and Ag2C2O4 by electrospinning technique and successive ionic layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) process (J. J. Li et al., 2003). The optical properties, surface morphology and crystalline structure of PN/Ag composite membranes have been studied through various characterization. Furthermore, the PN/Ag composite membranes were applied for the disinfection of E. coli and S. aureus under visible-light irradiation, 7.48 and 7.70 log inactivation of E. coli and S. aureus respectively were achieved within 80 min under the conditions of few catalysts, no additional oxidants and low energy consumption. Moreover, the reusability and possible photocatalytic mechanism of PN/Ag composite membranes were discussed in order to better understand the photocatalytic system. The successful strategy of this kind of plasmonic heterojunction photocatalysts may shed light on the design and application of materials in water purification.

Polyacrylonitrile (PAN, Mw = 150, 000) and isopropanol (IPA, 99.5%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Melamine (C3H6N6, 99.0%, AR), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5%, AR), citric acid (C6H8N7, 99.5%), urea (CH4N2O, 99%, AR), 4-hydroxy-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinyloxy (TEMPOL, 98%) and sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4, AR) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.8%) and potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7, 99.8%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co, Ltd, China. The above chemicals were all directly used without any further purification.

As reported in the previous work, NCDs were synthesized of citric and urea with a ratio of 3:1 via a hydrothermal method (Wang et al., 2017) The NCDs/g-C3N4 was synthesized by a facile polymerization method. In a typical experiment, 3.00 g of melamine was dissolved in 20.00 mL of deionized water containing 0.75 g of NCDs solution. After mechanical mixing, the mixture was evaporated at 70 °C. The resulting powder was then heated to 550 °C at a heating rate of 3.0 °C/min for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resultant pale-yellow powders were collected by grinding with an agate mortar.

The PN composite membrane was prepared by electrospinning. Firstly, 100 mg NCDs/g-C3N4 and 9.15 g DMF were ultrasonically mixed for 5 h. The NCDs/g-C3N4 were dispersed in the DMF solution by ultrasonic treatment. Then 0.75 g PAN were put into the mixture and mixed with a magnetic stirrer at room temperature (∼25 °C) for 12 h to obtain a uniform 8.5 wt% PAN/NCDs/g-C3N4 electrospinning solution. The electrospinning process was carried out using the Nanospider™ NS Lab 2G electrospinning lab unit (Elmarco, Liberec, Czech Republic) (Tan et al., 2018). The distance between the collecting electrode and the active electrode was 190 mm, while the voltage was 25 kV and the speed of the carriage was 200 mm/s with the temperature and relative humidity controlled 25 °C and 35%, respectively. Ultimately, the PN composite membrane was vacuum-dried at 60 °C for 12 h to remove the residual DMF.

PN/Ag composite membrane was fabricated via a SILAR method. Briefly, PN composite membrane was immersed in Na2C2O4 solution (0.05 M) and AgNO3 solution (0.1 M) for 5 min, respectively, then Ag2C2O4 was formed on the PN composite membrane. Subsequently, DI water was used to wash away free ions during each process. Finally, PN/Ag-X (X = 1, 5 and 10) composite membranes were obtained following treatment with an oven at 60 °C for 5 h in dark. X means different circulation times of SILAR process. Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of PN/Ag composite membranes were shown in Fig. 1 .

The morphologies of the samples were observed using scanning electron microscope (FESEM; Zeiss Merlin). Phase structures of different samples were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD，Bruker D8 Advance) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 5700 spectrometer with KBr pellets in the 500-4000 cm−1 region. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded by Shimadzu UV3600. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on a fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments FLs980) at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were carried out on a CHI 760D electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Chenhua Co, China) using a three-electrode cell. Electron spin response (ESR) signals with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as radical spin-trapped reagent were recorded on a Bruker EMXnano at room temperature.

The representative pathogenic bacteria were selected to examine the bactericidal activity of the as-prepared composite membranes, including Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922) and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 6538). The E. coli and S. aureus were first incubated in the LB broth at 37 °C for 12 h to prepare the bacterial suspension. Afterwards, the bacterial suspension was successively centrifuged and washed with PBS solution to obtain the bacteria concentration of 3 × 107 and 5 × 107 CFU mL−1, respectively.

In a typical photocatalytic disinfection experiment, 10 mg of the as-synthesized composite membrane was immersed into a 50 mL jacketed beaker containing 20 mL of the bacterial suspension, and a 300 W Xe lamp (PLS-SXE 300, Beijing Perfect light Co, Ltd) with a cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm) was applied as light source. At fixed time intervals, 0.5 mL of bacterial suspensions were collected and continuously diluted with PBS solution ( × 102, × 103, × 104, and × 105) and then added onto the agar plate for bacterial enumeration. The composite membranes were rinsed with ethanol and DI water after each test during cyclic disinfection experiment. All the disinfection experiments were carried out in triplicates.

The bacteria were harvested through centrifugal separation before and after photocatalytic disinfection, then fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde solution for 3 h and rinsed with PBS solution for three times. Subsequently, the bacterial samples were serially dehydrated by ethanol solution with various concentrations (30, 50, 70, 90, 100%). Ultimately, the bacterial samples were freeze-dried, sprayed with gold and for morphology observation by SEM.

The morphology of as-prepared materials was presented by using SEM. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, NCDs/g-C3N4 was formed of overlapping lamellar nanosheets. The surface of pure PAN membrane was smooth without any corrugations, which was shown in Fig. 2b. Interestingly, it was found in Fig. 2c that PN composite membrane had a rough and winkled surface, indicating NCDs/g-C3N4 nanosheets loaded on the surface of PAN membrane. As depicted in Fig. 2d, there were just only a few Ag2C2O4 nanosheets can be seen on PN composite membrane. However, with the increase of SILAR process, more and more Ag2C2O4 nanosheets were anchored on PN composite membrane and formed thorny morphology, demonstrating that the heterojunctions between NCDs/g-C3N4 and Ag2C2O4 can be formed possibly (Fig. 2e and f).

The XRD patterns of as-fabricated samples were observed in Fig. 3 a. The two distinct diffraction peaks at 2θ = 13.0° and 27.5° of NCDs/g-C3N4 were consistent with those reported previously, which were in accordance with the (1 0 0) and (0 0 2) planes of pure g-C3N4 (JCPDS No.87-1526) (Ye et al., 2013; J. Zhang, Zhang et al., 2014). The XRD pattern of Ag2C2O4 showed four peaks at 2θ = 17.18°, 19.11°, 29.72° and 32.36° indexed to the crystallite planes of (1 1 0), (2 0 0), (0 1 1) and (1 1 1) in monoclinic phase of Ag2C2O4 (JCPDS No.22-1335) (Feng et al., 2017). With the increase of SILAR process times, the diffraction peaks of Ag2C2O4 gradually increased, which indicates that the crystalline structure of Ag2C2O4 was well loaded on the composite membrane. For PN/Ag-10 and PAN/Ag-10, the diffraction peaks of PN/Ag-10 was stronger than PAN/Ag-10, demonstrating that the exist of NCDs/g-C3N4 was conducive to the construction of Ag2C2O4. Nevertheless, the diffraction peaks a of NCDs/g-C3N4 was absent from the XRD patterns of composite membrane due to the low amount of NCDs/g-C3N4.

As shown in Fig. 3b and Fig.S1, the FTIR spectra was used to analyze the surface group and structure of PAN, PN, Ag2C2O4 and PN/Ag-X composite membranes. The spectrum of PAN shows bands at 2923 cm−1, 2242 cm−1, 1627 cm−1, and 1451 cm−1 corresponding to –CH2 stretching, CN stretching, CC stretching and C–H bending vibration respectively, which was the main sign to identify PAN (Karbownik et al., 2015). After NCDs/g-C3N4 was added, the spectrum of the PN exhibited a slight shift, indicating an interaction between PAN matrix and NCDs/g-C3N4 nanosheets (Chaoran Zhang et al., 2019a,b). Meanwhile, the peaks at 810 cm−1 could be attributed to the breathing mode of triazine ring units (Cao et al., 2020). For the spectrum of pure Ag2C2O4, it is consistent with previous research reports (Abbasi_Abbasi et al., 2020). The absorption bands at 522 cm−1, 1304 cm−1, 1372 cm−1 and 1575 cm−1 can be assigned to the C–CO bending mode, COO−1 (carboxylate group), C–O stretching mode and CO stretching mode. Additionally, with the increase of Ag2C2O4, the peak intensity of 1304 cm−1 and 1575 cm−1 bands became stronger, indicating that the Ag2C2O4 had successfully introduced.

As shown in Fig. 4 a, the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the as-fabricated materials were collected. One could see that Ag2C2O4 showed significant absorption bands at 200–400 nm, which had a slightly visible-light response. In contrast to Ag2C2O4, the NCDs/g-C3N4 demonstrated a wide light absorption range in both visible and UV regions. It could be found that the adsorption of PN composite membrane showed a slight decrease compared to NCDs/g-C3N4, indicating the PAN nanofiber membrane was only the carrier of NCDs/g-C3N4, rather than embedded in the lattice of NCDs/g-C3N4 (Yuan et al., 2020). Moreover, with the increase of Ag2C2O4, the composite membranes showed obviously enhanced absorption in both UV and visible regions, which might be related to the heterojunction between NCDs/g-C3N4 and Ag2C2O4. Further, the band gap of the samples was obtained according to the equation (1):
(1)
αhυ
=
A
( hυ
- E
g
)
n/ 2
where α, h, Eg, A and υ are the absorption coefficient, Plank's constant, band gap, a constant and frequency of the incident photon, respectively (X. Wu et al., 2020). When it comes to NCDs/g-C3N4 and Ag2C2O4, n equals 1 and 4 for the other composite membranes in this work. The estimated band gap of NCDs/g-C3N4 and Ag2C2O4 were calculated to be 2.63 eV and 3.83 eV respectively, which was close to that reported in previous study (Feng et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017). The band gap of PN, PN/Ag-1, PN/Ag-5 and PN/Ag-10 were about 2.28, 2.24, 2.15 and 2.08 eV, respectively (Fig. 4b).

The value of Urbach energy depends on the nature of the semiconductor and on the degree of structural disorder in the crystal lattice, which can be calculated by the following formula:
(2)
α =
α 0
exp
(
hv/E u
)
Where α0 is a constant and Eu is Urbach energy which corresponds to the width of the absorption edge tail (Kim et al., 2020). The Urbach energy has been determined by taking inverse of the slope obtained from linear fitted data of ln (α) vs. hυ (Fig. S2) and results are listed in Table. S1. The Urbach energy of PN, PN/Ag-1, PN/Ag-5 and PN/Ag-10 were about 0.29, 0.33, 0.37 and 0.40 eV, respectively. The increasing trend in the Urbach energy of the samples with the increase of SILAR process times might be ascribed to structure due to the increase of Ag2C2O4, and is also correlated with the decrease of the band gap energy (Durgesh, Kumar, Sharma and Sharma, 2022).

The disinfection activity of the resultant photocatalysts under visible light was assessed by inactivating pathogenic bacteria (E. coli and S. aureus were selected as model bacteria). Antibacterial test of as-fabricated samples was conducted in dark for 80 min firstly, and the antibacterial efficiency was negligible, suggesting the composite membranes possessed quite minor cytotoxicity and adsorption to E. coli and S. aureus (Fig. S3). Similarly, under 80 min visible light irradiation, the antibacterial efficiency could be ignored without photocatalyst, demonstrating that there is no photolysis of bacterial cells under visible light irradiation. The result of photocatalytic disinfection performance of composite membranes for E. coli and S. aureus is shown in Fig. 5 . As illustrated in Fig. 5a and b, pure PAN membrane had no effect on E. coli and S. aureus inactivation under 80 min visible light irradiation because few bacteria were deposited on the surface of PAN membrane and its poor visible absorption (Gao et al., 2016). For PN composite membrane, only 0.50 and 0.96 log of CFU reduction for E. coli and S. aureus respectively were observed under visible light illumination for 80 min. For PAN/Ag-10 composite membrane, 1.09 and 2.47 log of CFU reduction for E. coli and S. aureus respectively were achieved under the same conditions. The corresponding photographs of E. coli and S. aureus colonies before and after photocatalytic disinfection were shown in Fig. 5e and f. As a sharp contrast, when the light was turned on in the presence of PN/Ag-1, PN/Ag-5 and PN/Ag-10 composite membranes, all E. coli (7.48 log) and S. aureus (7.70 log) were inactivated in 80 min. In particular, PN/Ag-10 composite membrane showed the highest disinfection activity and achieved 7.48 and 7.70 log CFU reduction of E. coli and S. aureus in 60 min, respectively. The bacteria plate paint photos of different disinfection time for E. coli and S. aureus were shown in Fig. S4. The result suggested that the photocatalytic activity of PAN membrane was significantly improved through heterojunctions between NCDs/g-C3N4 and Ag2C2O4, as well as the optimized SPR effect of Ag nanoparticles during visible light illumination (Fig. S5) (Awazu et al., 2008; D. Chen et al., 2013; Lee et al., 2017). The pH of a reaction system may affect the photocatalysis. Hence, the photocatalytic disinfection was carried out at various pH value. As shown in Fig. S6, the reduction of E. coli and S. aureus were significantly unaffected with the increase in pH from 3 to 9. Furthermore, the highest photocatalytic disinfection efficiency of PN/Ag-10 composite membrane appeared at pH 4.5 and 7.5. In addition, compared with other reported photocatalysts, the as-fabricated PN/Ag-10 composite membrane still exhibited higher visible-light disinfection activity (Table S2). However, it has been reported that Ag+ can interfere with cell division and cause bacterial cell death because of the photochemical reaction between Ag ions and the thiol group (-SH) of cysteine (Matsumura et al., 2003; Zeng et al., 2019). Moreover, only 0.9 log decrease of viable cell population induced by exposing to 100 μg/L Ag+ solutions under light irradiation conditions in previous study (Jin et al., 2013). As shown in Fig. S7a, the release of Ag ions from PN/Ag-10 composite membrane during photocatalytic in solution was about 29.5 μg/L. It could be found that this also has no effect on the antibacterial process (Fig. S7b).

Stability is an important index for the practical application of the composite membranes. To verify the disinfection stability of the composite membranes, PN/Ag-10 and PAN/Ag-10 composite membranes were used for long-term photocatalytic disinfection. As depicted in Fig. 5c and d, PN/Ag-10 kept steady photocatalytic disinfection activity after three and four continuous antibacterial experiments of E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. As exhibited in Fig. S8, it can be seen that the photocatalytic disinfection activity of PAN/Ag-10 significantly decreased after three and four continuous antibacterial experiments of E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. It means that the stability of photocatalyst was improved. Furthermore, Fig. S9 shows XRD patterns comparison of PN/Ag-10 before and after visible light irradiation, it can be seen that, after recycle experiments, the typical characteristic peaks of Ag at 38.2°, 44.2°, 64.4° and 77.4° appeared in addition to the diffraction peaks of Ag2C2O4, which is well consistent with SEM results (Fig. S5). This indicates that the appearance of Ag is helpful to improve the stability of photocatalyst (Feng et al., 2017). Moreover, the morphological changes of bacterial cells before and after the photocatalytic disinfection were observed by SEM (Fig. 6 ). As depicted in Fig. 6a and c, E coli possessed a rod-like shape, while S. aureus was globular with smooth surface respectively before the photocatalytic disinfection. Conspicuously, the cell walls of E. coli and S. aureus were damaged and the surfaces were collapsed after the photocatalytic disinfection by PN/Ag in Fig. 6b and d.

Reactive species which were generated under visible light irradiation, such as photogenerated holes (h+), photogenerated electrons (e−), hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and superoxide radicals (·O2 −), play important roles in photocatalytic disinfection (Y. Chen et al., 2011). The reactive species capture experiments were conducted to figure out the contributions of those reactive species to E. coli and S. aureus inactivation in PN/Ag-10 photocatalytic system with adding sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4), potassium dichromate (Cr (VI)), isopropanol (IPA) and TEMPOL as the quenchers of h+, e−, ·OH and ·O2 −, respectively (Liang et al., 2015; D. Wu et al., 2016).

Conspicuously, the inactivation of E. coli and S. aureus was inhibited most significantly by adding Cr (VI), demonstrating a dominant role of e− in the photocatalytic disinfection. At the same time, the addition of Na2C2O4 to quench h+ was found to be a moderate inhibition of E. coli and S. aureus inactivation, implying h+ can contribute to bacterial inactivation in the photocatalytic disinfection. Interestingly, with the addition of isopropanol for quenching ·OH, the disinfection efficiency of S. aureus was also observed a moderate reduction, but only a slight change for E. coli. Therefore, ·OH played moderate and minor roles in killing S. aureus and E. coli, respectively. Furthermore, the disinfection efficiency of E. coli showed a slight reduction with the addition of TEMPOL, suggesting that ·O2 − was also a minor role during E. coli inactivation process. Whereas the inactivation of S. aureus was almost unaffected in the presence of TEMPOL. In a conclusion, e− played a dominant role in killing both E. coli and S. aureus, while h+, ·OH and ·O2 − can contribute to the inactivation of E. coli to a certain extent. Moreover, h+ and ·OH also played important roles in the passivation of S. aureus (Fig. 7 a and b).

The spin-trapping ESR technique was widely used to evaluate the roles of reactive species in the photocatalysis process (Y. Yang et al., 2019). DMPO (5,5-diemthyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide) was employed as a spin trapping agent for ·O2 − and ·OH. As exhibited in Fig. 7c, there was no obvious signal in the dark for ·OH, whereas under visible-light illumination, typical quartet peaks of DMPO-·OH adduct were observed with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1, revealing the production of ·OH by the composite membrane. As can be seen in Fig. 7d, the spectra of DMPO-·O2 − adduct, in which the sextet peaks were formed under visible light irradiation, while only negligible signal could be seen in the dark, confirming that the generation of ·O2 − occurred. Hence, it could be inferred that ·OH and ·O2 − were involved in the bacterial inactivation, which was in good accordance with the conclusion of reactive species capture experiments.

In order to evaluate the separation and transfer efficiency of the surface charge carriers of as-prepared photocatalysts, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of as-synthesized composite membranes was measured. Generally, the low PL intensity indicates that the recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes is low (Zhou et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. 7e, the PN composite membrane exhibited a strong emission peak at around 440 nm. However, the intensity of the emission peak decreased sharply with Ag2C2O4 nanosheets were loaded on the surface of PN composite membrane, suggesting that the heterojunctions between Ag2C2O4 and NCDs/g-C3N4 could effectively enhance the separation rate of charge carriers. The time-resolved photoluminescence spectra further confirmed the efficient charge separation. It can be seen that the value of average lifetimes of the carriers (τavg) are 2.59 and 2.65 ns for the PN and PN/Ag-10, respectively (Fig. S10 and Table. S3). The increased τavg indicates that forming heterojunction help prolong the photogenerated charge lifetime (Zhao et al., 2019). The ·OH radical contents generated over PN/Ag-10 under visible light irradiation were investigated via fluorescence spectrum of 2,4-dicarboxilic acid at different periods of time (Dashtian et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. S11, the terephthalic acid suspension, along with the PN/Ag-10 photocatalyst, shows an increase in the PL intensity with the increase of the time of visible light irradiation which represents the increasing concentration of active ·OH radical species (Amiri et al., 2019). Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) is an effective method to evaluate the charge-transfer resistance and separation capability of electron-hole pairs. In general, the smaller the radius of the Nyquist impedance diagram, the lower the charge transfer resistance between the electrolyte solution and the working electrode interface (Yang et al., 2017; X. Yang et al., 2019). As depicted in Fig. 7f, PN/Ag-10 composite membrane possessed the lowest charge-transfer resistance when compared with PN, PN/Ag-1 and PN/Ag-5 composite membranes, demonstrating that the heterojunctions between Ag2C2O4 and NCDs/g-C3N4 give the materials faster charge transferability. Hence, this inference was well in accordance with the PL spectra.

Above the preceding measurements and explanations, we proposed the band gap structure of the PN/Ag composite membranes. When NCDs/g-C3N4 was composited with Ag2C2O4, the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) potentials of the semiconductor could be theoretically calculated as the following equations (Nethercot, 1974):
(3)
E VB
=
X
-
E C
+
0.5
E g
(4)
E CB
=
E VB
−
E g
where X is the electronegativity of the semiconductor, EC is the energy of free e− on the hydrogen scale, which is generally considered to be about 4.5 eV, and Eg is the band gap energy of the semiconductor. The Eg of NCDs/g-C3N4 and Ag2C2O4 could be procured by the UV–vis DRS were about 2.63 and 3.83 eV, respectively. The X values for the as-synthesized NCDs/g-C3N4 and Ag2C2O4 were 4.72 and 6.31 eV, respectively (Feng et al., 2017; Jiao et al., 2019). Hence, the ECB and EVB of NCDs/g-C3N4 can be obtained, which were −1.10 and + 1.53 eV, while were −0.11 and + 3.72 eV for Ag2C2O4, respectively.

Based on the above results, the potential Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanisms for inactivation of E. coli and S. aureus over PN/Ag composite membranes were proposed in Fig. 8 . In the system of PN/Ag, the photogenerated electrons and holes were produced by NCDs/g-C3N4, Ag nanoparticles and Ag2C2O4 under visible light illumination. Interestingly, the Ag nanoparticles work as the charge transmission-bridge in the system of visible-light-driven PN/Ag. On the one hand, the electrons on the CB of Ag2C2O4 were captured by Ag nanoparticles to recombine with the holes generated by Ag nanoparticles through the Schottky barrier in the metal-semiconductor interface, which resulted in efficient separation of electron-hole pairs of Ag2C2O4. Simultaneously, the photogenerated holes remained in the VB of Ag2C2O4 can react with H2O/OH− to generate ·OH with strong oxidizing power because the EVB of Ag2C2O4 (+3.72 eV vs. NHE) is more positive than the potential of ·OH/H2O (+2.73 eV vs. NHE) and ·OH/OH− (+1.99 eV vs. NHE). On the other hand, the plasmon-induced electrons of Ag nanoparticles can easily inject into the CB of NCDs/g-C3N4 to reduce the dissolved oxygen to ·O2 −, as the ECB of NCDs/g-C3N4 (−1.10 eV) is more negative than O2/·O2 − (−0.33 eV vs. NHE). Furthermore, the holes remained in NCDs/g-C3N4, Ag2C2O4 and Ag nanoparticles can direct damage the E. coli and S. aureus. Therefore, the cooperative effects of NCDs/g-C3N4, Ag2C2O4 and Ag nanoparticles contributed to effectively separate electron-hole pairs and produced more reactive species, leading to the efficient photocatalytic disinfection of PN/Ag composite membranes under visible light illumination.

In summary, a novel composite membrane (PN/Ag) with plasmonic heterojunction was synthesized for the efficient photocatalytic disinfection through the combination of PAN, NCDs/g-C3N4 and Ag2C2O4 by electrospinning technique and SILAR process. Moreover, the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of Ag nanoparticles and Schottky barrier formation between metal and semiconductor contributed to the efficient separation of electron-hole pairs and the generation of reactive species, resulting in outstanding photocatalytic disinfection of PN/Ag composite membranes (7.48 and 7.70 log inactivation of E. coli and S. aureus respectively in 80 min) and good reusability under visible light illumination. We hope that the composite membranes with improved can be used as the photocatalytic antibacterial materials in applications of environment purification.

Jiaxin Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiuquan Lan: Validation, Investigation. Jianhua Cheng: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Xinhui Zhou: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financed by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976060), the Social Science and Technology Development Project of Dongguan (No. 2019507163434) and the Introduced innovative R&D team leadership of Dongguan city (No. 2020607263005).
